title: Pre-Payment Penalties & Privileges Explained
metaTitle: Prepayment Penalties in Canada — IRD vs 3 Months' Interest
metaDescription: How Canadian mortgage prepayment penalties really work. IRD vs 3 months' interest, posted-rate vs discounted-rate IRD, and how to avoid surprises at break.
intro: Most Canadian mortgage holders don't understand their prepayment penalty until they need to break. By then it's too late to choose differently. Here's how it works — and how to choose lenders whose math is friendlier.

body.heading: Two penalty calculations
paragraphs: On a fixed-rate mortgage, you pay the greater of three months' interest or the Interest Rate Differential (IRD). On a variable-rate mortgage, almost always just three months' interest. That's why variable-rate holders have far more flexibility to break early.
Three months' interest is straightforward: your remaining balance × your interest rate × 3/12. On a $700,000 mortgage at 5%, that's about $8,750.

IRD is the killer. It compares your contract rate to the lender's current rate for a similar-remaining-term product, and charges you the rate difference × balance × remaining term. If rates dropped 1% since you signed, with 3 years left on a $700K mortgage, IRD ≈ $21,000.

body.heading: Posted-rate IRD vs. discounted-rate IRD
paragraphs: Big banks (RBC, TD, BMO, Scotia, CIBC, National) compute IRD using their posted rate — the inflated headline number they advertise — even though you got a discount off it. This produces dramatically larger penalties. Monoline lenders (MCAP, First National, Merix, RFA) and most credit unions compute IRD using your actual discounted rate, producing penalties typically 60–80% lower for the same scenario.

On a typical $700K Greater Vancouver mortgage with 3 years left, big-bank IRD might run $25,000–$40,000 while monoline IRD on the identical scenario runs $5,000–$12,000. That's the most expensive convenience charge in Canadian banking.

body.heading: Prepayment privileges
paragraphs: Most lenders allow 10–20% of original balance to be prepaid annually without penalty, plus payment increases of 10–20%. Choose the highest privilege you can — paying down principal without penalty knocks years off your amortization. Privileges generally don't carry over year to year; use them or lose them.

faqs.question: How do I find out my exact penalty?
faqs.answer: Request a written 'payout statement' from your lender. Verbal quotes can be off by thousands. The written quote is good for 30 days.

faqs.question: Can I avoid IRD by waiting?
faqs.answer: Yes — within the last 90 days of your term, most lenders waive the penalty entirely. Even before that, the IRD calculation amount shrinks as remaining term gets shorter.

faqs.question: Should I choose a lender just for the penalty calculation?
faqs.answer: If there's any chance you'll break early — divorce, relocation, refinance, sale within 5 years — yes, factor penalty structure into your lender choice. Saving 10 bps on rate but adding $20K to potential penalty is a bad trade.
